Pankaj Tibrewal of Ikigai Asset on Channel Checks 

On-ground checks amid ongoing global tensions — what we are seeing so far:

Over the past few days, our team connected with multiple companies across sectors to understand how the current situation is unfolding at the ground level. A few key observations:

1. Demand remains resilient across sector till now 

Encouragingly, there is no visible demand destruction so far. Most of sectors in-fact said demand has been quite decent till now. For eg: most auto ancillary companies indicated that OEM schedules for even April–May remain healthy. However, given recent price increases across sector, some moderation in demand over the coming months cannot be ruled out.

2.Margins holding up (for now)

Margins remain stable as companies are still consuming lower-cost inventory.Price hikes have been meaningful across sectors, and most corporates are proactively securing raw material supplies to ensure business continuity. However if situation persist then margin headwind could be see in 1Q and part could be negated by higher top-line growth. 

3. Shift towards shorter-term contracts:

There is a clear preference for shorter-duration contracts—fortnightly or monthly—on both raw materials and finished goods. With uncertainty around commodity prices, companies are avoiding long-term commitments at elevated levels.

4. Unorganised sector under pressure, market share shift to leaders across sectors 

Smaller players are facing challenges due to working capital constraints and raw material volatility. This is leading to a gradual shift in market share towards stronger, well-capitalised leaders.

5. Labour availability becoming a constraint for MSMEs:

Several companies highlighted emerging labour challenges. In certain pockets, workers are returning to their villages due to issues like LPG availability, upcoming elections, and lingering fears of a Covid-like disruption. This is beginning to impact operations across small scale industries.

6. Logistics disruptions increasing lead times:

Logistics is becoming a bottleneck, with fuel availability at highways turning inconsistent. Delivery timelines, which were earlier around 4 days, are now stretching to 7–8 days in many cases. Freight rates from China to India has gone by 2x. 

7. Chemicals sector seeing tailwinds:

The chemical sector continues to witness strong demand. Supply pressures from China have eased due to logistics disruptions and currency movements, supporting better spreads. However, raw material availability remains a constraint.

8. Petrochemical chain seeing availability constraints:

The broader petrochemical value chain is witnessing both availability constraints and pricing volatility, creating uncertainty across multiple downstream industries.

9. Innovation and adaptability on display

Encouragingly, companies are responding with agility. For instance, some AC/EMS players that were dependent on gas cylinders for welding and paint shops are now shifting to alternative processes like oxy-acetylene, helping mitigate supply disruptions and maintain continuity.

10. Preparing for structurally higher energy costs

Many corporates believe that even if the war subsides, oil prices may remain elevated. Countries are likely to build strategic reserves after this energy shock, keeping demand firm. As a result, the entire oil value chain could see sustained inflation—creating top-line tailwinds for several sectors, even as cost pressures persist.

11.Inflation: not all bad for earnings

A moderate level of inflation is often supportive for corporate earnings and nominal GDP growth, as it drives higher realisations and revenue growth. While there could be some pressure on margins and currency in the near term, the overall setup remains constructive.

Periods like these often accelerate market share gains for stronger players, while also setting the stage for nominal growth tailwinds in an inflationary environment.

Management meet takeaways,

Domestic demand momentum remains healthy, especially in the 350cc segment, where demand currently outstrips supply.

Fuel tax changes announced by the gov’t today provide meaningful relief to the OMCs while capping refining upsides for RIL

Following are changes that have been announced

1) Cut in excise duty on petrol & diesel by Rs10/ltr (not passed through to retail prices, lowering marketing losses for the OMCs)

ONGC: INDIA’S $20 BILLION DEEP-SEA ENERGY BET UNDER “SAMUDRA MANTHAN”  

KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
• ONGC committing ~$20B toward deep-sea oil & gas exploration  
• Strategic shift: energy security becomes national priority  
• Focus on offshore basins, especially Andaman region  

INDIA’S ENERGY DEPENDENCE (CORE PROBLEM)  
• ~88% crude oil imports  
• ~60% LPG imports  
• Significant reliance on imported natural gas  
• High vulnerability to geopolitical shocks (West Asia, Hormuz)  

STRATEGIC OBJECTIVE  
• Reduce import dependency  
• Build long-term energy independence  
• Hedge against global supply disruptions  

SAMUDRA MANTHAN VISION  
• Large-scale offshore hydrocarbon exploration program  
• Targeting untapped deep-water reserves  
• Integrating energy, logistics & blue economy strategy  

OFFSHORE FOCUS (KEY OPPORTUNITY)  
• Andaman basin: high-potential hydrocarbon reserves  
• Comparable geology to Indonesia (~700K barrels/day output)  
• Potential upside: up to ~2M barrels/day (long term)  

TECHNOLOGY & EXECUTION  
• Deployment of advanced deep-sea rigs (15–20 storey structures)  
• Use of:  
  – Floating rigs  
  – Robotic submersibles  
  – Underwater drones  
• Global partners shortlisted: BP, ExxonMobil, TotalEnergies, Petrobras  
• Aggressive timeline: infrastructure mobilization within ~80 days  

ONSHORE PARALLEL EXPLORATION  
• Ganga basin (Ballia, Samastipur, Prayagraj)  
• Drilling depth up to ~3,000 meters  
• Limited scale vs offshore opportunity  

ENERGY LOGISTICS STRATEGY  
• ₹6 lakh Cr annual spend on energy transportation  
• Push for domestic gas carriers (Cochin Shipyard & others)  
• Goal: reduce import logistics cost + strengthen maritime capability  

INFRA & BLUE ECONOMY INTEGRATION  
• Andaman & Nicobar development:  
  – Transshipment ports  
  – Expanded runways  
  – Urban infrastructure  
• Integration with:  
  – Trade routes  
  – Energy transport  
  – Rare earth exploration  

STRATEGIC INSIGHT  
• Shift from “energy importer” → “resource developer” mindset  
• Offshore exploration = multi-decade structural play  
• Combines energy security + geopolitics + industrial policy  

RISKS  
• Exploration uncertainty (reserves may not scale as expected)  
• High capex intensity & long gestation period  
• Execution complexity in deep-sea environments  
• Dependence on global technology partners  

IMPACT  
• Positive (Long-term) – transformational move toward energy security, with potential to significantly reduce import dependence and strengthen India’s strategic positioning

SEMICONDUCTOR PACKAGING & SILICON PHOTONICS: THE REAL AI BOTTLENECK  

KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
• AI performance bottleneck shifting from compute → data movement  
• Packaging & interconnects emerging as key performance drivers  
• Silicon photonics likely next major breakthrough in AI infrastructure  

CORE PROBLEM (AI INFRA LIMITATION)  
• AI workloads need massive parallel compute + ultra-fast communication  
• GPUs scaling rapidly, but data transfer becoming bottleneck  
• Latency, bandwidth & power efficiency now critical constraints  

ADVANCED PACKAGING SHIFT  
• Technologies: 2.5D/3D packaging, chiplets, fan-out packaging  
• Enables higher compute density & better power efficiency  
• Industry shift:  
  – Earlier: Transistor scaling drove performance  
  – Now: Packaging + architecture driving performance  

INTERCONNECT BOTTLENECK  
• Copper-based interconnect limitations:  
  – High power consumption  
  – Heat generation  
  – Bandwidth constraints  
• Even advanced packaging cannot fully solve data transfer challenges  

SILICON PHOTONICS (NEXT FRONTIER)  
• Uses light instead of electrons for data transfer  
• Key benefits:  
  – 10–100x higher bandwidth  
  – Lower power consumption  
  – Scalable for hyperscale data centers  
• Applications: GPU-to-GPU, server-to-server, AI cluster connectivity  

CRITICAL CHALLENGE: PHOTONICS PACKAGING  
• Integration of optics + electronics is highly complex  
• Requires micron-level precision in alignment & laser coupling  
• Thermal management & manufacturing complexity extremely high  
• Represents highest entry barrier in semiconductor value chain  

AI STACK EVOLUTION  
• Traditional: Compute (chips) driven  
• Emerging:  
  Power → Data Center → Servers → GPUs →  
  Packaging → Photonics Packaging → AI Models  
• Packaging now core performance layer, not backend  

INDIA OPPORTUNITY  
• Realistic entry point: Semiconductor packaging (not advanced fabs)  
• Direct players: Tata Electronics, CG Power, SPEL Semiconductor  
• Ecosystem support: Kaynes Technology, Dixon Technologies  
• Niche exposure: izmo Ltd. (photonics-linked capabilities)  

STRATEGIC INSIGHT  
• Future winners will be companies enabling high-speed data movement  
• Packaging + interconnect innovation = next semiconductor cycle driver  

IMPACT  
• Positive (Long-term) – structural shift creates new high-value opportunity beyond traditional chip manufacturing

HOLLOW CORE FIBER (HCF): NEXT-GEN AI INFRASTRUCTURE THEME

KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
• AI bottleneck shifting from compute → data movement  
• Traditional fiber: light through glass (higher latency, energy loss)  
• HCF: light through air → faster, efficient, scalable  

TECH ADVANTAGE (WHY HCF MATTERS)  
• ~30–50% lower latency  
• Near vacuum-speed data transmission  
• Lower power consumption (fewer repeaters)  
• Higher bandwidth with minimal signal distortion  

---

STRUCTURAL IMPACT ON AI ECOSYSTEM  

1. AI SCALING UNLOCK  
• Faster GPU-to-GPU communication across geographies  
• Enables distributed AI training clusters  

2. DATA CENTER SHIFT  
• DCs can move closer to cheap renewable power sources  
• Reduces dependency on urban locations  

3. ENERGY EFFICIENCY  
• Lower transmission losses → lower infra + energy cost  
• Critical as AI power demand explodes  

4. TELECOM CAPEX CYCLE  
• Potential multi-year upgrade cycle in fiber networks  
• Similar to 5G-led telecom capex wave  

---

INDIA OPPORTUNITY  
• Currently import-dependent in optical technologies  
• HCF creates fresh domestic manufacturing + R&D opportunity  
• Early-stage theme → long runway  

---

POTENTIAL BENEFICIARIES (INDIA)  

OPTICAL / TELECOM CORE PLAYERS  
• HFCL – Developing HCF tech with IIT Delhi  
• Sterlite Technologies – Global fiber leader  
• Tejas Networks – Optical networking & transmission  

ECOSYSTEM PLAYERS  
• Aksh Optifibre – Optical fiber niche player  
• Polycab India – Cables & last-mile infra  
• KEI Industries – Infra + transmission cables  

---

STRATEGIC VIEW  
• AI is evolving into a full-stack infra story:  
  Power → Data Centers → GPUs → Networks → Fiber  
• HCF sits at the network + data movement layer  
• Early adoption phase → high optionality, high uncertainty  

---

RISKS  
• Technology still evolving → commercialization timeline uncertain  
• High capex requirements for large-scale rollout  
• Competition from incremental improvements in existing fiber tech  

---

BIG PICTURE  
• Next decade winners may not just be chipmakers  
• Data movement enablers (fiber, networks) could become silent compounders  
• HCF = potential backbone of global AI infrastructure  

---

IMPACT  
• Positive (Long-Term Theme) – Early-stage but high-impact technology with potential to drive next telecom & AI infra capex cycle

• Lucknow facility emerges as one of the world’s largest titanium recycling centres  
• Capacity scaled from zero to global scale via single strategic acquisition  
• Positions PTC in high-value aerospace & defence supply chain  

THE STRATEGIC STORY  
• Global titanium supply highly concentrated: China + Russia control ~74%  
• Major OEM dependency: Boeing (~80%) & Airbus (~60%) on Russia  
• Post Ukraine war:  
  – Lead times surged ~5x  
  – Prices jumped ~160%  
• Created urgent global need for alternative supply → recycling became critical

• Acquired 5,000 TPA EBCHR furnace from Aubert & Duval  
• Purchase price: ~₹125 Cr vs original cost ~₹430 Cr  
• Massive capital efficiency + entry into high-barrier segment  

WHY SUCH A LOW COST?  
• Geopolitical shift → relocation of strategic manufacturing assets  
• Preference for “friendly” countries like India  
• Strong policy support:  
  – UP Defence Industrial Corridor  
  – Backing from UPEIDA  

GROWTH DRIVERS  
• Titanium recycling = strategic necessity globally  
• Strong demand from aerospace, defence, and advanced engineering  
• Import substitution + global export opportunity  

FINANCIAL / STRATEGIC IMPACT  
• Entry into niche, high-margin titanium value chain  
• Significant cost advantage due to discounted capex  
• Potential for strong operating leverage as utilization ramps up  

STRATEGIC VIEW  
• Classic case of opportunistic capital allocation during geopolitical disruption  
• Creates long-term moat in critical materials supply chain  
• Positions PTC as key player in global titanium ecosystem  

IMPACT  
• Positive – Strategic acquisition + global supply shift creates strong long-term growth and competitive advantage

KMEW's fleet runs at 100% utilisation, with dredgers operating 270 to 300 days a year. Its order book has swelled to ₹1,500 crore. It is leaner and moves much faster than DCI.

But the cost of India's port expansion is not just financial. It is also borne by the people who live along the coast.

At Vizhinjam, on Kerala's southern coast, the construction of the international seaport has been linked to faster erosion along nearby fishing villages.

The port's large stone barriers disrupted the natural movement of sand, starving beaches to the north. Protests turned violent, with around 80 people injured. The port authorities say they followed all environmental clearances. The fishers say the clearances were not enough.

A few kilometres north, at Muthalapozhi, the situation is the opposite. Fishers there have demanded regular dredging of the harbour mouth because dangerous siltation has capsized boats. Their demand is not "stop dredging" but "dredge more, and dredge here too."

In one place, the port is the problem. In another, only more dredging can fix what the port created.

So here is where things stand. DCI's first new dredger arrives by June 2026. Ten more are in the pipeline. KMEW is planning ₹183 crore in fleet expansion and has entered shipbuilding. The Kolkata subsidy dispute remains unresolved. And on Kerala's coast, fishers and port authorities still argue over who bears the cost of a changing shoreline.

The mud will keep coming back, as it always does. What is less certain is whether India is building the systems to deal with it well.

Knowledge Marine and Engineering Works, or KMEW, was founded in 2015. It is the only other publicly listed dredging company in India, with a younger fleet focused on river and inland dredging.

It is a fraction of DCI's size, but far more focused on profitability.

Old dredgers mean higher fuel consumption, lower productivity, frequent breakdowns, and disqualification from contracts that set age limits on equipment.

This is the context behind the ₹4,000 crore fleet overhaul. The first new dredger, 12,000 cubic metre capacity, is being built at Cochin Shipyard and expected by June 2026.

But even with 11 new vessels, DCI's fleet will still be modest against the scale of work India is planning.

Meanwhile, a much smaller private company is growing fast in the same space.

For the longest time, dredging in India has been a public monopoly led by the Dredging Corporation of India.

Established in 1976, DCI dominates maintenance dredging at major ports. But its fleet is old. The average vessel age is over 20 years. Some have exceeded their useful life entirely.

But dredging is not just about scooping soft mud. It involves surveying the seabed, cutting or sucking up sediment, transporting it, dumping or reusing it, and surveying again to confirm depth. Every step adds to the cost.

And the seabed does not always cooperate.

A Mumbai Port Trust feasibility study found that dredging soft soil costs roughly ₹110 per cubic metre. Dredging rock costs ₹4,000 per cubic metre. A 36-fold difference.

An unexpected rock layer beneath the seabed can blow a project's budget by orders of magnitude.

The government's own 2021 Dredging Guidelines warn that disputes frequently arise from inadequate soil surveys and wrong dredger selection. Contractors bid based on the port's survey. If that survey has gaps, costs inflate beyond what is on paper. This often ends up in court.

Then there is the payment problem. The government has reportedly not paid over ₹1,000 crore in dredging subsidies to the Kolkata Port, approved over a decade ago. The Ministry says profitable ports should fund dredging themselves.

But the port's counter is simple: if they bear the cost, they pass it on to exporters and importers through higher shipping charges. Trade suffers either way. A common problem in infrastructure where the government is the biggest customer.

The port has two docks: one at Kolkata, one 100 km away at Haldia. Ships heading to Haldia sometimes have to partially unload cargo at neighbouring ports first because the river is too shallow.

On the other end of the country, the Cochin port faces a different version of this fight.

India is also becoming more ambitious. It wants bigger ships at its ports, and bigger ships need deeper channels.

Under Sagarmala, the government has identified 839 projects worth ₹5.79 lakh crore aimed at port-led development. A significant chunk involves deepening channels. All of it starts with moving sediment.

Every monsoon, the river mouth silts up heavily. At ports like these, dredging is not a one-time job. It is a permanent running cost, like electricity or wages. Stop for a season and the channel becomes too shallow for commercial ships to pass through.

Clear sand, silt, and rock from the bottom of harbours, rivers, and shipping channels so that large ships can pass through.

Government estimates suggest India will need roughly 3 billion cubic metres of dredging over the next decade. Every major port depends on it to stay open.

Between 2017 and 2019, India's major ports moved roughly 159 million cubic metres of sediment. Some ports need it more desperately than others.

The Kolkata port is the most dramatic example.

It sits on the Hooghly estuary, where the river carries enormous quantities of silt from the Ganga system, depositing it across the shipping channel constantly. The port spends ₹300 to 500 crore annually just fighting this.

And it is still not always enough.

India just committed ₹4,000 crore to a business most people have never thought about: dredging.

The Dredging Corporation of India wants to more than double its revenue to ₹3,000 crore over five years. It is buying 11 new dredgers. All to do one thing.

IRAN UNDERGROUND BASE: HIGHLY FORTIFIED BUT NOT IMPENETRABLE  

KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
• Deep underground missile base built within hard granite formation  
• Facilities located hundreds of meters below surface  
• Multi-layer tunnels, rail systems & multiple exits enhance survivability  
• Designed to withstand conventional airstrikes  

STRUCTURAL STRENGTH  
• Granite significantly stronger than standard reinforced concrete  
• Thick rock layers absorb and dissipate explosive energy  
• Deep placement reduces effectiveness of direct penetration weapons  

WEAPON LIMITATIONS  
• Bunker busters have limited penetration depth in hard rock  
• Single-strike impact unlikely to reach deeply buried core facilities  
• Effectiveness depends on strike angle, geology & repeated targeting  

REALITY CHECK  
• Complete destruction is difficult but not necessary in warfare  
• Surface infrastructure, entry/exit points & logistics can be targeted  
• Repeated strikes or alternative methods can degrade functionality  

STRATEGIC VIEW  
• Objective typically to disable operations, not destroy entire structure  
• Combination of military, cyber & strategic tactics used in such scenarios  

IMPACT  
• Neutral – strong defensive capability, but not fully immune to modern warfare strategies
